bibliographic_data.title: Observation of Unidirectional Bound States in the Continuum Enabled by Topological Defects
bibliographic_data.abstract: Unidirectional radiation is important for a variety of optoelectronic applications. Many unidirectional emitters exist, but they all rely on the use of materials or structures that forbid outgoing waves, i.e., mirrors. Here, we theoretically propose and experimentally demonstrate a class of resonances in photonic crystal slabs, which only radiate towards a single side with no mirror placed on the other side — we call them “unidirectional bound states in the continuum”. These resonances are found to emerge when a pair of half-integer topological charges in the polarization field bounce into each other in the momentum space. We experimentally demonstrate such resonances in the telecommunication regime, where we achieve single-sided quality factor as high as 1.6 × 10^5, equivalent to a radiation asymmetry ratio of 27.7 dB.

core_content.novelty_claim: Theoretical proposal and experimental demonstration of unidirectional bound states in the continuum (BICs) enabled by topological charges in the polarization field of photonic crystal slab resonances.
core_content.key_methodology: Splitting an integer topological charge into a pair of half-integer topological charges, which then bounce into each other to form unidirectional BICs.
outcomes: Theoretical and experimental demonstration of unidirectional BICs in photonic crystal slabs.
Achieved single-sided quality factor as high as 1.6 × 10^5.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Unidirectional radiation is crucial for various optoelectronic applications, but existing unidirectional emitters rely on materials or structures that block outgoing waves (mirrors). This paper proposes and experimentally demonstrates a new class of resonances in photonic crystal slabs called 'unidirectional bound states in the continuum' (BICs), which radiate only towards one side without any mirror. These BICs arise when half-integer topological charges in the polarization field bounce into each other in momentum space. The authors experimentally demonstrate these resonances in the telecommunication regime, achieving a single-sided quality factor of up to 1.6 × 10^5 and a radiation asymmetry ratio of 27.7 dB.

nodes.label: Unidirectional Bound States in the Continuum (BICs)
